metadata.summary: pytest-django is the preferred testing framework for Django projects, combining pytest's powerful fixtures, parametrization, and cleaner syntax with Django's database and request handling.

content.title: Django Testing with pytest
content.summary: pytest-django is the preferred testing framework for Django projects, combining pytest's powerful fixtures, parametrization, and cleaner syntax with Django's database and request handling. This methodology covers test organization, fixtures, mocking, Factory Boy integration, and performance optimization.

why.p: pytest-django bridges pytest's modern testing philosophy with Django's framework conventions. Compared to Django's native TestCase, it offers explicit modular fixtures, built-in parametrization, a 800+ plugin ecosystem, and cleaner assertion syntax. The same fixture system works for unit tests, API integration tests, and performance-sensitive parallel suites. Fixtures compose explicitly (one test declares its dependencies), making tests self-documenting and reducing hidden setup coupling.

list.item: Writing unit tests for Django models, services, and utilities
Setting up test data with Factory Boy
Pure unit tests of helpers/pure functions with no Django dependency — plain pytest (no pytest-django) is faster and clearer
One-off scripts, ad-hoc manage.py shell exploration — fast and disposable beats fixture infrastructure
Tests that must run inside manage.py test (legacy CI gates, third-party plugins assuming Django runner) until that constraint is removed

section.name: core-concepts
section.p: pytest-django provides a few core fixtures and markers that bridge pytest to Django's ORM and request handling.

rule.statement: Any test that accesses the database MUST be marked with @pytest.mark.django_db. Fixtures that create database objects MUST explicitly request the 'db' fixture. Use transactional_db only when the test asserts on transaction commit/rollback semantics or post-save signals.
rule.rationale: The db fixture wraps each test in a transaction and rolls back after the test, ensuring test isolation. Omitting the marker causes "no database access allowed" errors. Mismatching db and transactional_db creates flaky tests that depend on transaction semantics but aren't marked for it.

example.title: Correct database fixture usage
example.note: The db fixture is injected as a parameter, granting database access to the fixture. The function is marked to access the database.

example.title: Missing db fixture or marker
example.note: Either error causes the test to fail with database access errors or test isolation violations.

section.name: fixture-types
section.p: pytest-django provides several database fixtures with different transaction semantics. Choosing the correct one prevents flaky tests and TransactionManagementError exceptions.

rule.statement: Use 'db' fixture for all tests by default (wraps test in auto-rollback transaction). Use 'transactional_db' only when testing explicit transaction behavior (transaction.atomic, post-commit hooks, select_for_update). Use 'django_db_reset_sequences' when testing auto-increment ID generation. Use 'django_db_serialized_rollback' for multi-database setups.
rule.rationale: The 'db' fixture isolates each test in a transaction, so modifications do not persist to other tests. transactional_db disables this rollback, allowing the test to see real committed changes but requiring explicit cleanup. Mismatching creates silent failures where one test pollutes another's state or signals don't fire when expected.

example.title: Fixture types in use

section.name: factory-boy-integration
section.p: pytest-factoryboy bridges Factory Boy factories with pytest fixtures, eliminating manual test data creation and enabling composable, reusable test objects.

rule.statement: Define Django model factories in tests/factories/ using factory.django.DjangoModelFactory. Use factory.SubFactory for foreign keys, factory.Faker for realistic data, and @factory.post_generation for complex setup. Register factories in conftest.py via pytest_factoryboy.register() to auto-create 'model' and 'model_factory' fixtures.
rule.rationale: Factories decouple test data creation from test logic. pytest-factoryboy auto-creates two fixtures per factory (singular instance and _factory for batch creation), reducing boilerplate. SubFactory and Faker make factories self-maintaining as schemas evolve.

example.title: Factory Boy setup
example.code:
# tests/factories/users.py import factory from apps.users.models import User
class UserFactory(factory.django.DjangoModelFactory): class Meta: model = User skip_postgeneration_save = True # Django 4.2+ optimization
email = factory.Faker("email") name = factory.Faker("name") user_type = "regular" is_active = True
@factory.post_generation def password(self, create, extracted, **kwargs): self.set_password(extracted or "testpass123") if create: self.save(update_fields=["password"])
# tests/conftest.py from pytest_factoryboy import register from .factories.users import UserFactory
register(UserFactory) # Creates 'user' and 'user_factory' fixtures
# Usage in test @pytest.mark.django_db def test_user_creation(user): assert user.email is not None assert user.pk is not None

section.name: directory-structure
section.p: Organizing tests into unit, integration, and e2e directories with shared conftest.py makes tests maintainable and self-documenting.

rule.statement: Organize tests as: tests/conftest.py (root fixtures), tests/factories/ (Factory Boy classes), tests/fixtures/ (complex fixtures), tests/unit/ (unit tests, no DB), tests/integration/ (DB + API), tests/e2e/ (end-to-end). Each subdirectory MAY have its own conftest.py with directory-specific fixtures.
rule.rationale: This structure scales from small projects to monorepos. Unit tests run fastest (no DB), integration tests share factories, conftest inheritance (root → unit/ → module) manages fixture scope cleanly. Developers immediately know where a test belongs and what setup it uses.

example.title: Recommended directory structure
example.code: tests/ ├── conftest.py # Root fixtures (shared across all tests) ├── factories/ │ ├── __init__.py │ ├── users.py # UserFactory, ProfileFactory │ └── orders.py # OrderFactory, OrderItemFactory ├── fixtures/ │ ├── __init__.py │ ├── api.py # API client fixtures │ └── data.py # Data fixtures ├── unit/ │ ├── conftest.py # Unit-specific fixtures │ ├── test_models.py │ ├── test_services.py │ └── test_validators.py ├── integration/ │ ├── conftest.py # Integration-specific fixtures │ ├── test_api.py │ └── test_views.py └── e2e/ ├── conftest.py └── test_flows.py

section.name: api-testing
section.p: Testing Django REST Framework endpoints requires authenticated API clients, parametrized payloads, and permission assertions.

rule.statement: Create authenticated API client fixtures using APIClient.force_authenticate(user=...). Test each endpoint for: 401 (anonymous), 403 (wrong permissions), 200/201 (happy path), 400 (validation), 404 (not found). Use parametrize for validation test matrices. Always use format='json' and assert response.json() shape, never browsable API HTML.
rule.rationale: API tests need to verify all security layers (authentication, authorization, validation). Parametrized test matrices cover edge cases without test duplication. format='json' enforces API contract, not HTML rendering.

example.title: DRF API testing

section.name: performance-optimization
section.p: Django test suites can become slow. Parallel execution, database reuse, and session-scoped fixtures dramatically improve runtime.

rule.statement: Run tests in parallel with pytest-xdist: pytest -n auto. Use --reuse-db to skip migrations on subsequent runs (cold start: --create-db). Use MD5PasswordHasher in test settings to skip expensive bcrypt hashing. Session-scope immutable fixtures (categories, static data) to avoid re-setup. Mark slow tests with @pytest.mark.slow and run separately in CI.
rule.rationale: Parallel execution scales with CPU cores (2-4x speedup on quad-core). Database reuse skips migration cost (savings: 20-50% on test suites with many apps). Faster password hashing saves per-user-creation cost (bcrypt ~200ms → MD5 ~1ms). Session-scoped fixtures amortize one-time expensive operations.

example.title: Performance optimization
example.code:
# config/settings/test.py PASSWORD_HASHERS = [ "django.contrib.auth.hashers.MD5PasswordHasher", # Fast for tests ]
CELERY_TASK_ALWAYS_EAGER = True # Run Celery tasks synchronously
# conftest.py - Session-scoped fixture for expensive setup @pytest.fixture(scope="session") def sample_products(django_db_setup, django_db_blocker): """Create immutable product data once per test session.""" with django_db_blocker.unblock(): return [ Product.objects.create(name="Laptop", price=999.99), Product.objects.create(name="Mouse", price=29.99), ]
# CLI - Run tests in parallel with database reuse pytest -n auto --reuse-db --cov

section.name: mocking-external-services
section.p: Tests isolate business logic from external dependencies using unittest.mock, responses library, and freezegun.

rule.statement: Mock external service calls at the boundary (API calls, not internal functions). Use unittest.mock.patch for function mocks, responses library for HTTP mocks, freezegun for datetime mocks. Always verify mock was called with the expected parameters. Reset mocks between tests (autouse fixtures handle this).
rule.rationale: Mocking isolates code under test from flaky/slow external services. Boundary mocking (API client, not internal _validate functions) tests real behavior. Verifying calls ensures integration contract is maintained.

example.title: Mocking external services

section.name: advanced-patterns
section.p: More complex patterns for real-world Django testing scenarios.

rule.statement: For complex fixture setup, compose fixtures explicitly by requesting multiple fixture parameters. Use pytest_plugins to modularize fixtures across subdirectories. Session-scoped fixtures MUST be immutable — create new copies for each test unless explicitly intending shared state.
rule.rationale: Explicit fixture composition makes dependencies visible. pytest_plugins lets domain-specific fixtures live near domain-specific tests. Session-scoped fixtures save setup time for expensive one-time operations (loading data), but immutability is critical to prevent test pollution.

example.title: Composed fixture pattern

rule.statement: For performance-sensitive suites, use session-scoped fixtures for immutable test data (categories, static products). Use --reuse-db and MD5PasswordHasher in test settings. Mark slow tests with @pytest.mark.slow and run separately in CI. Profile test duration with pytest --durations=10.
rule.rationale: Session-scoped fixtures amortize one-time expensive operations (e.g., loading 1000 product categories). --reuse-db skips migration cost. MD5PasswordHasher is ~200x faster than bcrypt for tests. Profiling reveals unexpected slowdowns and prevents regression.

example.title: Performance optimization in settings and fixtures
example.code:
# config/settings/test.py PASSWORD_HASHERS = [ "django.contrib.auth.hashers.MD5PasswordHasher", # Fast for tests ] CELERY_TASK_ALWAYS_EAGER = True # Run Celery tasks synchronously
# tests/conftest.py @pytest.fixture(scope="session") def sample_products(django_db_setup, django_db_blocker): """Create immutable product data once per test session.""" with django_db_blocker.unblock(): return [ Product.objects.create(name="Laptop", price=999.99), Product.objects.create(name="Mouse", price=29.99), Product.objects.create(name="Keyboard", price=79.99), ]
# tests/integration/test_checkout.py @pytest.mark.django_db def test_checkout_with_sample_products(user, sample_products): """Fast test using pre-created session-scoped products.""" cart = Cart.objects.create(user=user) for product in sample_products: cart.items.create(product=product, quantity=1) total = cart.calculate_total() assert total == sum(p.price for p in sample_products)

section.name: checklist
section.p: Pre-commit verification checklist for Django tests.

checklist.check: All tests marked with @pytest.mark.django_db if they access the database
Module-level pytestmark = pytest.mark.django_db for modules with 100% DB tests
Factories created for every model; registered via pytest_factoryboy.register
APIClient fixtures use force_authenticate, not credentials()
All response assertions check status code AND response.data shape
format='json' used in all API requests (never browsable API HTML)
Permission tests use @pytest.mark.parametrize for all role combinations
External service calls mocked at the boundary (API client, not internal functions)
No bare except: or over-broad except Exception:
Patch() targets use the import site (apps.services.send_mail), not definition site
pytest -n auto runs tests in parallel without failures
No test interdependencies (each test is independent)

section.name: common-patterns
section.p: Proven patterns for organizing test logic and avoiding anti-patterns.

rule.statement: Use Arrange-Act-Assert (AAA) pattern: Arrange test data, Act on the unit under test, Assert the result. Test behavior, not implementation (verify outcomes, not internal method calls). One logical assertion per test (multiple asserts on the same concept are OK; asserts on different concerns split into separate tests). Compose fixtures explicitly; avoid hidden state in class attributes.
rule.rationale: AAA clarifies test intent. Testing behavior makes tests survive refactoring. One assertion focus prevents tests from failing for multiple reasons, making failures easy to diagnose. Explicit fixture composition makes test dependencies visible.

example.title: Test behavior, not implementation
example.code:
# Good: Testing observable behavior def test_create_order_rejects_empty_cart(user): with pytest.raises(ValidationError, match="Cart is empty"): service.create_order(user, items=[])
# Good: Arrange-Act-Assert def test_order_total_calculation(order_factory, product): # Arrange order = order_factory(items=[{"product": product, "quantity": 2}])
# Act total = order.calculate_total()
# Assert assert total == product.price * 2
# Good: Composed fixtures @pytest.fixture def order_with_payment(order_factory, payment_factory): """Order with completed payment for testing fulfillment.""" order = order_factory(status="pending") payment_factory(order=order, status="completed") return order

antipattern.description: Testing internal implementation details: mocking _validate() helper, asserting on internal method calls, testing private attributes.
antipattern.reason: Implementation tests break when refactoring. Observable behavior tests survive refactoring. Mock the API boundary (API client, database), not internal functions.

antipattern.description: Shared mutable state in class attributes or session-scoped factories. Tests interfere with each other or depend on execution order.
antipattern.reason: Fixture scopes must match mutation semantics. Function-scoped fixtures (default) ensure test isolation. Session-scoped fixtures only for immutable data.

template.purpose: pytest configuration in pyproject.toml with Django settings, coverage, and markers
Root conftest.py with API client, database, and Factory Boy fixtures
Service unit test template with mocking and parametrization
DRF API integration test template with authentication and validation
Factory Boy factory definitions for models with SubFactory and Faker
